perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party may disclose Confidential Information if so required by judicial or administrative process s, provided that it shall promptly notify the Disclosing Party so as to allow the Disclosing Part y reasonable time to oppose such process, and provided that Receiving Party shall minimize su ch disclosure to the extent possible.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party may disclose Confidential Information if required by a court order, and notification to the Disclosing Party is at the Receiving Party's discretion. The Receiving Party is not obligated to minimize such disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: Modifying the obligation to notify the disclosing party and minimize disclosure contradicts standard legal practices surrounding court orders and confidentiality.
perturbation.law_explanation: FRCP 45 outlines the procedures for subpoenas, including the right of a party to object and seek to quash or modify the subpoena if it requires disclosure of privileged or protected matter. Removing the notification requirement hinders the Disclosing Party's ability to protect its confidential information as permitted under FRCP 45. The duty to minimize disclosure aligns with the principle of proportionality in discovery, which the changed text disregards.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 45. Subpoena | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 45. Subpoena (a) In General. (1) Form and Contents. (A) Requirements—In General. Every subpoena must: (i) state the court from which it issued; (ii) state the title of the action and its civil-action number; (iii) command each person to whom it is directed to do the following at a specified time and place: attend and testify; produce designated documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things in that person's possession, custody, or control; or permit the inspection of premises; and (iv) set out the text of Rule 45(d) and (e). (B) Command to Attend a Deposition—Notice of the Recording Method. A subpoena commanding attendance at a deposition must state the method for recording the testimony. (C) Combining or Separating a Command to Produce or to Permit Inspection; Specifying the Form for Electronically Stored Information. A command to produce documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things or to permit the inspection of premises may be included in a subpoena commanding attendance at a deposition, hearing, or trial, or may be set out in a separate subpoena. A subpoena may specify the form or forms in which electronically stored information is to be produced. (D) Command to Produce; Included Obligations. A command in a subpoena to produce documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things requires the responding person to permit inspection, copying, testing, or sampling of the materials. (2) Issuing Court. A subpoena must issue from the court where the action is pending. (3) Issued by Whom. The clerk must issue a subpoena, signed but otherwise in blank, to a party who requests it. That party must complete it before service. An attorney also may issue and sign a subpoena if the attorney is authorized to practice in the issuing court. (4) Notice to Other Parties Before Service. Secure .gov websites use HTTPS A lock ( Lock Locked padlock icon ) or https:// means you’ve safely connected to the .gov website. Share sensitive information only on official, secure websites. Federal Rules of Civil Procedure The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure govern civil proceedings in the United States district courts. The purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure is "to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding." Fed. R. Civ. P. 1. The rules were first adopted by order of the Supreme Court on December 20, 1937, transmitted to Congress on January 3, 1938, and effective September 16, 1938. The Civil Rules were last amended in 2024. perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the requirements for the Receiving Party to notify the Disclosing Party of a court order and to minimize disclosure of Confidential Information. The Law Explanation states that removing the notification requirement hinders the Disclosing Party's ability to protect its confidential information as permitted under FRCP 45, and that the duty to minimize disclosure aligns with the principle of proportionality in discovery. Scraped Law Snippet 1 refers to Rule 45, which relates to subpoenas and the process for disclosing information. While Snippet 1 doesn't explicitly state notification requirements in all cases, the Law Explanation, coupled with the removal of notification and minimization requirements in the changed text, indicates a contradiction of the legal principles and processes associated with protecting privileged information during discovery. By making notification discretionary and removing the obligation to minimize disclosure, the changed text weakens protections for confidential information that the law aims to provide.

perturbation.original_text: All Confidential Information is and shall remain the property of the Disclosing Party. The parties acknowledge that all Confidential Information is the sole property of the Disclosing Party and that the Recei ving Party shall not acquire any proprietary interest in the Confidential Informatio n. All applicable rights to mask works, topographies, patents, copyrights, trademarks and t rade secrets with respect to the Confidential Information are retained exclusively b y the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: All Confidential Information is and shall remain the property of the Disclosing Party, except for any improvements or derivative works created by the Receiving Party. The parties acknowledge that all Confidential Information is the sole property of the Disclosing Party, but the Receiving Party may acquire a joint proprietary interest in any derivative works created using the Confidential Information. All applicable rights to mask works, topographies, patents, copyrights, trademarks and t rade secrets with respect to the Confidential Information are initially retained exclusively b y the Disclosing Party, but may be jointly owned for derivative works.
perturbation.explanation: Introducing the concept of joint ownership of derivative works contradicts the initial assertion that all confidential information remains the sole property of the disclosing party, and creates ambiguity regarding ownership of intellectual property.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification potentially contradicts copyright law (17 U.S. Code § 201) regarding ownership. While derivative works can be copyrighted, the original copyright holder retains rights to the underlying work. By suggesting joint ownership of all rights related to derivative works, the modified text creates a conflict with the original owner's rights and the established legal framework for copyright ownership.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Chapter 2 - Circular 92 | U.S. Copyright Office Home Copyright Law of the United States (Title 17) Chapter 2 Chapter 2: Copyright Ownership and Transfer PDF Version (94 KB) Back to top 201. Ownership of copyright 1 (a) Initial Ownership.—Copyright in a work protected under this title vests initially in the author or authors of the work. The authors of a joint work are coowners of copyright in the work. (b) Works Made for Hire.—In the case of a work made for hire, the employer or other person for whom the work was prepared is considered the author for purposes of this title, and, unless the parties have expressly agreed otherwise in a written instrument signed by them, owns all of the rights comprised in the copyright. (c) Contributions to Collective Works.—Copyright in each separate contribution to a collective work is distinct from copyright in the collective work as a whole, and vests initially in the author of the contribution. In the absence of an express transfer of the copyright or of any rights under it, the owner of copyright in the collective work is presumed to have acquired only the privilege of reproducing and distributing the contribution as part of that particular collective work, any revision of that collective work, and any later collective work in the same series. (d) Transfer of Ownership.— (1) The ownership of a copyright may be transferred in whole or in part by any means of conveyance or by operation of law, and may be bequeathed by will or pass as personal property by the applicable laws of intestate succession. (2) Any of the exclusive rights comprised in a copyright, including any subdivision of any of the rights specified by section 106 , may be transferred as provided by clause (1) and owned separately. The owner of any particular exclusive right is entitled, to the extent of that right, to all of the protection and remedies accorded to the copyright owner by this title. (e) Involuntary Transfer.—When an individual author’s ownership of a copyright, or of any of the exclusive rights under a copyright, has not previously been transferred voluntarily by that individual author, no action by any governmental body or other offici
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces the concept of joint ownership of derivative works, which contradicts the initial statement that all confidential information remains the sole property of the disclosing party. This directly undermines the ownership rights as defined in copyright law. Scraped Law Snippet 1, citing 17 USC 201, states that copyright initially vests in the author or authors of the work. While it also acknowledges co-ownership for joint works, the original contract clearly designates the disclosing party as the sole owner. The modification creates a contradiction by suggesting joint ownership where the initial agreement stipulates sole ownership. Scraped Law Snippet 2 further supports this by stating that copyright vests initially in the author, and in the case of joint work, the authors are co-owners. By allowing the receiving party to acquire a joint proprietary interest, the modified text contradicts the established framework for copyright ownership as it relates to the originally defined 'Confidential Information'.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party shall treat the Confidential Information with the same degree of confidentiality as it keeps its own confidential information, but in all events no less than a reaso nable degree of confidentiality.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party shall treat the Confidential Information with the same degree of confidentiality as it keeps its own confidential information, regardless of whether that degree of confidentiality is reasonable.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the 'no less than a reasonable degree of confidentiality' clause allows the Receiving Party to handle the Confidential Information with potentially inadequate security measures, even if their own standards are subpar.
perturbation.law_explanation: The UTSA defines misappropriation to include the acquisition of a trade secret by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means. The modified text removes the explicit requirement for a "reasonable degree of confidentiality," potentially allowing the Receiving Party to handle the information with a standard of care that falls below what is considered reasonable, thus increasing the risk of misappropriation. A reasonable standard of confidentiality would be expected to be upheld in a legal proceeding.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Uniform Trade Secrets Act - Wikipedia Jump to content From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia Uniform act in the United States Intellectual property Authors' rights Copyleft Copyright Database right Farmers' rights Geographical indication Indigenous intellectual property Industrial design right Integrated circuit layout design protection Intellectual property infringement Literary property Moral rights Patent Peasants' rights Plant breeders' rights Plant genetic resources Proprietary software Related rights Supplementary protection certificate Trade dress Trade secret Trademark Utility model Patent infringement Related topics Abandonware Artificial intelligence and copyright Brand protection Copyright abolition Copyright troll Criticism of copyright Bioprospecting Biopiracy Idea–expression distinction Limitations and exceptions to copyright Fair dealing Fair use Paraphrasing Right to quote Orphan work Patent troll Pirate Party Public domain Outline of intellectual property Outline of patents Higher categories: Property and Property law v t e The Uniform Trade Secrets Act ( UTSA ), published by the Uniform Law Commission (ULC) in 1979 and amended in 1985, is a model law designed for adoption by U.S. states . [ 1 ] It was developed to resolve inconsistencies in the treatment of trade secrets across different states. [ 1 ] (p1) The UTSA provides unitary definitions for "trade secret" and "trade secret misappropriation" and establishes a single statute of limitations, replacing the varied property, quasi-contractual, and fiduciary liability frameworks previously applied under common law . [ 1 ] (p2) To date, 49 states, the District of Columbia, Puerto Rico, and the U.S. Virgin Islands have adopted the UTSA, with New York as the remaining exceptions. [ 2 ] Motivation [ edit ] A prefatory note to the UTSA states some original motivations for the act: A valid patent provides a legal monopoly for seventeen years in exchange for public disclosure of an invention. If, however, the courts ultimately decide that the Patent Office improperly issued a patent, an invention has been disclosed to competitors with no corresponding benefit. In view of the substantial number of patents that the courts invalidate, many businesses now elect to protect commercially valuable information by relying on the state trade secret protection law. — Uniform Trade Secrets Act, Prefatory Note [ 3 ] The UTSA made note of the commercial value and competitive advantages inherent in trade secrets. Unlike patent protection, which was addressed at the federal level, trade secret misappropriation was addressed at the state level. [ 3 ] In the United States there existed a prevalence of interstate commercial transactions that extended beyond the jurisdiction of individual state legislation. For example, goods may have been manufactured in State A, warehoused in State B, sold from State C, and delivered in State D. As a result, the UTSA sought to alleviate the uneven development and "uncertainty concerning the parameters of trade secret protection" by recommending a uniform trade secret law and, at the same time, allowing the states the flexibility to meet local circumstances by modifying the text as enacted in eac
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text removes the requirement for a 'reasonable degree of confidentiality,' potentially leading to inadequate security measures. The Law Explanation states that this increases the risk of misappropriation, which the UTSA addresses. Scraped Law Snippet 2 mentions that the UTSA provides unitary definitions for 'trade secret' and 'trade secret misappropriation'. By removing the 'reasonable' standard, the Receiving Party could handle the information with a standard of care below what is legally expected, making misappropriation more likely and thus contradicting the intent of the UTSA to protect trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: Either party may terminate this Agreement at any time by written notice to the other party. Upon such termination or other expiration of this Agreement, the Receiving Party will return all of the Confidential Information in written or other tangible form, including any copies made, to the Disclosing Party together with certification that any other copies or notes or summaries (to the extent i ncluding the Confidential Information) have been destroyed. The provisions of Sections 1 t hrough 5 of this Agreement shall survive any termination or expiration.
perturbation.changed_text: Either party may terminate this Agreement at any time by written notice to the other party. Upon such termination or other expiration of this Agreement, the Receiving Party will return some of the Confidential Information in written or other tangible form, including some copies made, to the Disclosing Party without certification of any kind. The provisions of Sections 1 t hrough 5 of this Agreement shall not survive any termination or expiration.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'all' to 'some,' removing the certification requirement, and stating that provisions 1 through 5 shall not survive termination significantly weakens the protections for the Disclosing Party and undermines the purpose of the NDA.
perturbation.law_explanation: This change undermines the enforceability of the agreement, especially concerning injunctive relief. The Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 359 addresses the availability of specific performance and injunctions as remedies. By removing the survival clause for sections 1-5 (which include injunctive relief), the agreement loses its ability to prevent further misuse of confidential information post-termination, potentially contradicting established contract law principles regarding equitable remedies for breach of contract.

perturbation.justification: The modified text significantly weakens the protections for the Disclosing Party by changing 'all' to 'some' regarding the return of confidential information and removing the certification requirement. More critically, it states that provisions 1-5 shall not survive termination. The Law Explanation states that removing the survival clause contradicts established contract law principles regarding equitable remedies for breach of contract, as it loses the ability to prevent further misuse of confidential information post-termination. While the provided law snippets are not directly applicable, the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation highlight how the altered text undermines the enforceability of the agreement, creating a legal contradiction as it deviates from standard practices of protecting confidential information after termination.
